response.content: Solid-State Battery News: Samsung & Toyota | Monolith Monolith News Battery Testing New developments in solid-state batteries: Samsung & Toyota View all blogs In today’s rapidly evolving world, the quest for efficient energy sources is crucial to power our ever-expanding range of devices. For quite some time, lithium-ion batteries have been a significant player in energy storage. However, a compelling new competitor has emerged: solid-state batteries. Let's compare lithium-ion batteries with solid-state batteries to gain insight into future energy storage technology for electric vehicles (EVs), considering key developments in other chemistry batteries from companies like Samsung and Toyota. What’s the difference between solid state batteries and lithium-ion batteries? The primary distinction between solid-state batteries and traditional lithium-ion batteries lies in their electrolyte composition. While lithium-ion batteries use a liquid or gel electrolyte to facilitate the movement of lithium ions between the anode and cathode, solid-state batteries employ a solid electrolyte instead of the liquid. ( Source ) Four major differences between lithium and solid-state batteries: Cost: Solid-state batteries are currently more expensive because they are new and not as easy to manufacture in large quantities. In contrast, lithium-ion batteries, which have been around longer, are cheaper to produce. Energy storage: Solid-state batteries can store more energy per unit of weight and size than lithium-ion batteries. Currently, lithium-ion batteries store 250 to 300 Wh/kg, while solid-state batteries can store over 500 Wh/kg. Distance : Solid-state batteries can store more power, allowing vehicles to travel longer distances on a single charge. However, they take longer to recharge, which may not be preferred by some people. Safety : Solid-state batteries are safer because they don't contain the liquid that can cause fires or explosions in lithium-ion batteries. The evolution of
